title: Arts & crafts: Strong random unitaries and geometric locality
authors: Marten Folkertsma, Lorenzo Grevink, Jonas Helsen, Alicja Dutkiewicz
abstract: We study the problem of constructing strong approximate unitary $k$-designs on $D$-dimensional grids (and more generally on Cartesian products of graphs), building on the work of Schuster et al. arXiv:2509.26310 which establishes strong unitary designs in 1D and in all-to-all connectivity. We provide two constructions. The first construction leverages the existing all-to-all connectivity result with general routing theory to provide flexible (but slightly suboptimal) strong $k$-designs in arbitrary connectivities. The second construction is more direct, requires no auxiliaries and has provably optimal depth (in the number of qubits $n$) for $D$-dimensional grids with constant dimension. Combining these techniques also allows us to construct strong pseudorandom unitaries on $D$-dimensional grids with provably optimal depth.
summary: This paper provides efficient methods for building high-quality random quantum circuits on constrained hardware architectures. It achieves optimal circuit depth scaling for unitary k-designs on D-dimensional grids and shows that pseudorandom unitaries can be implemented with polylogarithmic depth.
main_problem: Determining the minimum circuit depth required to construct strong unitary k-designs and strong pseudorandom unitaries (PRUs) on quantum architectures with limited, geometric connectivity.
main_result: The authors provide two constructions for strong k-designs on D-dimensional grids, including one with provably optimal depth of O(n^(1/D)), and demonstrate that strong PRUs can be constructed with polylogarithmic depth.
method: The paper uses a 'strong gluing lemma' to compose small designs into larger ones and employs general routing theory to map all-to-all connectivity designs onto arbitrary grid-like graphs.
model_or_system: Quantum circuits acting on n qubits arranged in D-dimensional grids or more general Cartesian products of graphs, representing architectures like superconducting qubits, silicon quantum dots, and NV centers.
key_observables: Circuit depth (d), Total Variation (TV) distance, and trace distance between the constructed ensemble and the Haar measure.
important_parameters: Number of qubits (n), dimension of the grid (D), design strength (k), approximation error (epsilon), and the Learning with Errors (LWE) hardness assumption.
main_assumptions: The construction of strong PRUs assumes the sub-exponential post-quantum hardness of the Learning with Errors (LWE) problem; certain routing-based constructions require a large number of auxiliary qubits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of depth scaling for k-designs, presents a direct construction using a gluing lemma for optimal depth, provides a routing-based construction for arbitrary connectivity, and concludes with the construction of strong PRUs and discussion of complexity trade-offs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
